title: Horse-Powered Tracks: The Surrey Iron Railway
story: Clop-clop! Imagine horses pulling wagons along iron tracks. That's how the Surrey Iron Railway worked when it opened in south London in 1803. On July 26, this groundbreaking transport system began moving goods between towns. While not steam-powered like later trains, it paved the way for the railway revolution that would soon change the world.
activity_question: What pulled the wagons on the Surrey Iron Railway?
bonus_fact: The tracks were made of iron, which is why it was called an 'Iron Railway'.

title: Frank Chester: Cricket's Legendary Umpire
story: Frank Chester stood tall on the cricket field one last time. On July 26, 1955, this legendary umpire officiated his final Test match. Chester had a keen eye for the game, making fair calls that players respected. His career spanned decades, earning him a reputation as one of cricket's finest officials.
activity_question: What was Frank Chester known for in cricket?

title: Baseball Legends Join Hall of Fame
story: Cheers erupted in Cooperstown, New York as three baseball greats stepped into the spotlight. On July 26, 1987, pitcher Jim 'Catfish' Hunter, outfielder Billy Williams, and third baseman Ray Dandridge were inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame. These talented players had thrilled fans for years with their skills on the diamond. Now, their names would be forever enshrined alongside the sport's greatest heroes.

title: Explorer IV: America's Space Race Leap
story: Zoom! The US Army blasted off another space pioneer in 1958. Explorer IV soared into orbit on July 26, becoming America's fourth successful satellite. This metal marvel joined the space race, helping scientists learn about Earth's mysterious radiation belts. Each launch brought new discoveries and pushed the boundaries of what humans could achieve beyond our planet.

title: Canada's Space Leap: Anik D1 Takes Flight
story: Zoom! A powerful Delta rocket blasted off, carrying Canada's Anik D1 communications satellite into orbit. This exciting launch on July 26, 1982, marked a big step for Canada's space program. Anik D1 would help connect people across the vast Canadian landscape, beaming TV shows, phone calls, and data from coast to coast.

title: Rushing Waters Spark Massive Evacuation
story: Floodwaters surged in Cambodia after a dam collapsed in neighboring Laos in 2018. On July 26, authorities in Stung Treng province sprang into action. They quickly evacuated about 25,000 people living downstream, moving them to safer ground. The swift response showed how communities can work together to keep people safe during emergencies.

title: Britain's Bold Move for Cleaner Roads
story: Vroom! The roar of engines might soon fade in Great Britain. In 2017, the country made a big announcement on July 26. They decided to ban the sale of new gasoline and diesel cars by 2040. title: A Bold Step for Equality in the Military
story: President Harry Truman made a brave decision in 1948. On July 26, he signed a special order to make the US military fair for everyone. This order said all soldiers should be treated equally, no matter their skin color. It was a big step towards ending unfair treatment in the armed forces.

title: A Law for Equal Access
story: Wheels rolled across the White House lawn as President George H. W. Bush signed a groundbreaking law in 1990. The Americans with Disabilities Act opened doors for millions, ensuring fair treatment in jobs, schools, and public spaces. On July 26, Bush's pen stroke marked a new era of inclusion, making ramps, braille signs, and closed captions commonplace across the USA.

title: Ben Franklin Delivers a New Postal Service
story: Zip! Letters would soon travel faster across the colonies. In 1775, the Continental Congress created the United States Post Office in Philadelphia. They put Benjamin Franklin in charge on July 26. This clever inventor and statesman had already run the colonial mail system. Now, he'd help connect the new nation through speedy communication.
activity_question: Who was put in charge of the new United States Post Office?
bonus_fact: Franklin had previously invented a special odometer to measure postal routes.

title: Breaking Barriers in Education
story: Patrick Francis Healy made history in 1865. He became the first African American to earn a PhD from the University of Leuven in Belgium. This achievement opened doors for future generations. Healy's success on July 26 showed that with determination, anyone could reach the highest levels of education, no matter their background.

title: Lights, Camera, Action: The First Movie Theater
story: Popcorn and big screens weren't always part of our lives. In 1896, a groundbreaking event changed entertainment forever. Vitascope Hall, the world's first permanent movie theater that charged admission, opened its doors in New Orleans on July 26. This pioneering cinema paved the way for the movie-going experience we enjoy today.
activity_question: What was special about Vitascope Hall?
activity_choices: It was the first permanent for-profit movie theater
It was the largest theater in New Orleans
It had the biggest popcorn machine
activity_answer: It was the first permanent for-profit movie theater
follow_up_question: How do you think watching movies has changed since 1896?

title: Maldives Breaks Free: A Nation Reborn
story: Waves of change swept across the Indian Ocean as the Maldives declared its independence. On July 26, 1965, this island paradise said goodbye to British rule and hello to freedom. The Maldivians celebrated their newfound sovereignty, raising their distinctive red, green, and white flag high. It was a momentous day that marked the birth of the Republic of Maldives.
activity_question: What did the Maldives declare on July 26, 1965?

title: A War on Paper Finally Ends
story: Handshakes replaced hostility as the Netherlands officially ended its state of war with Germany in 1951. Though fighting had stopped years earlier, this July 26 declaration marked the formal close of a difficult chapter. It opened the door for friendlier relations between the two European neighbors, helping pave the way for future cooperation.
